channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. Launches New Strikes on Iran, Military Says
item.description: U.S. Central Command said on social media that it was protecting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump said earlier that he thought a three-week-old cease-fire was “over.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Trump Praises NATO’s ‘Unity’ After Lashing Out at Allies
item.description: Though President Trump assailed partners for not joining the war in Iran, he said he would grant Ukraine long-sought authority to make the Patriot defense system.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: At Iran’s Funeral for Ayatollah Khamenei, Mourners Bid an Emotional Farewell
item.description: Millions of Iranians descended on Tehran to pay their respects to the slain supreme leader.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Le Pen Launches Presidential Campaign, in a Twist for French Politics
item.description: The day after a court decided the far-right leader Marine Le Pen could run for office again, cheers and boos met her campaign kickoff in a rural stronghold.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen greeting supporters as she arrives on Wednesday for a campaign event in La Flèche, France.

item.title: Internal File Finds I.C.C. Prosecutor Engaged in Sexual Harassment
item.description: The confidential report will be considered by International Criminal Court members as they vote on whether to remove the top official.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.media:description: Karim Khan, the International Criminal Court’s chief prosecutor, at a meeting of the United Nations Security Council last year.

item.title: Trump’s Arrival in Turkey for NATO Summit Adds Drama and Spectacle
item.description: The second President Trump landed in Turkey, the center of gravity shifted right to where he likes it best: himself.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and Tyler Pager
item.media:description: President Trump with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey after arriving in Ankara on Tuesday.

item.title: As More Remains Are Found in Rubble From Venezuela Earthquakes, Horror Becomes Routine
item.description: Fearful that officials would tear down damaged buildings without accounting for any remaining bodies, Venezuelans are desperately digging for their relatives’ remains.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: A priest and nuns blessing the rubble of a collapsed building in Catia La Mar on Tuesday.

item.title: Heartbreak and Anger in Egypt Over a Stinging World Cup Loss to Argentina
item.description: After watching Egypt lead for almost the entire match, a last-minute victory by Argentina has driven fans to tears and conspiracy theories.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Rania Khaled
item.media:description: Egyptian football fans in Cairo react on Tuesday at the end of their national team’s World Cup loss to Argentina in the round of 16.

item.title: The Alluring Albanian Island Inspiring Ivanka’s Fantasy
item.description: A former military base in the Adriatic Sea, the island is in a beautiful setting but it is strewed with snakes, crumbling buildings and land mines.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins
item.media:credit: Ilir Tsouko for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of Sazan Island, once the site of Italian, Soviet and then Albanian military bases, now in ruins.

item.title: Marine Le Pen’s Return Has Sidelined Her Protégé. Can They Join Forces?
item.description: For months, Jordan Bardella was set to be the French far-right’s candidate for the presidency — until his mentor, Marine Le Pen, nudged him aside this week. Their relationship could define the campaign.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler

item.title: Police Raids on Ukrainian Drone Maker Fuel Fears About Press Freedom
item.description: The raids targeted a military contractor and its owner, who also co-owns a news outlet that recently exposed abuse of soldiers. Journalists said it was no coincidence.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Jędrzej Nowicki for The New York Times
item.media:description: An exhibition stand in Kyiv, Ukraine, last month, for Vyriy, the drone company that was raided on Tuesday.

item.title: Iraqis Turn Out in Droves to Mourn Khamenei and Show Solidarity
item.description: In Najaf and Karbala, home to two of Shiite Islam’s holiest sites, hundreds of thousands joined commemorations for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who extended Iran’s influence in Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sergey Ponomarev
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: After Insulting Allies at NATO Summit, Trump Says They Love Him
item.description: The meeting in Turkey featured President Trump at his most mercurial, veering from scorn to praise for European countries, including some whose patience with mollifying him has worn thin.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Farage’s Resignation Risks Becoming Farce as U.K. Parties Boycott Clacton By-Election
item.description: Under investigation over an undisclosed gift, the right-wing leader Nigel Farage may end up in a special election against a candidate dressed as a trash can.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: “I’ve decided that the people of Clacton should be the judges of my actions,” Nigel Farage, the leader of the Reform U.K. party, said as he announced his resignation as a lawmaker on Tuesday in London.

item.title: At the Ayatollah’s Funeral
item.description: A Times reporter in Iran said the events “surpassed anything I’ve ever witnessed.”
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times

item.title: Iran Truce ‘Over,’ Trump Says, as He Threatens More Strikes
item.description: A wave of U.S. attacks were followed by Iranian strikes, halting ship traffic in the Strait of Hormuz again. Oil prices rose sharply.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Tyler Pager, Aaron Boxerman and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: As Erdogan Hosted NATO, His Jailed Challenger Was in Court
item.description: President Recep Tayyip Erdogan basked in praise from President Trump with the spotlight on a NATO summit. At home, his biggest rival defended himself before judges.
item.dc:creator: Safak Timur and Ben Hubbard
item.media:description: Ekrem Imamoglu, the former mayor of Istanbul, during a political rally in 2024, before his detention.

item.title: Spread of Seafloor Directly Observed for the First Time
item.description: The spread of the ocean floor, as tectonic plates spread apart, is known but hard to observe. Scientists have now documented the process in action.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:description: Île Saint-Paul, a tiny island in the Indian Ocean, some 120 miles southwest of the deep sea site where the Antarctica and Australia tectonic plates diverged during an earthquake in 2024.

item.title: License to Make Patriot Missiles Could Help Ukraine Defend Against Russia
item.description: With Patriot interceptors in short supply, President Trump’s statement that he would allow Ukraine to build them is a boon to Kyiv as it fights off Russian missile attacks. But it is just the start.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut, Nicholas Kulish and John Ismay
item.media:description: A Patriot air defense system in Ukraine, in 2024. President Volodymyr Zelensky has warned for weeks that his country was running out of interceptors for the system as Russia has stepped up attacks.

item.title: Hotter Seas in Britain and Europe Threaten Marine Life
item.description: Higher sea temperatures have followed scorching weather across Europe, prompting Britain’s weather service to declare a “severe” marine heat wave.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:description: People diving into the English Channel in Brighton, on the south coast of England, during a heat wave last month.

item.title: Air Canada Chooses Anko van der Werff, Who Speaks French, as CEO After Language Backlash
item.description: Michael Rousseau, who prompted widespread criticism with a largely English condolence video following a crash in March, will retire.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Anko van der Werff, who will take over as chief executive of Air Canada in the fall, in Copenhagen last month.

item.title: Global Warming Could Ravage the Amazon’s Useful Plant Species, Study Finds
item.description: Hotter temperatures and harsher droughts could cause Indigenous societies to lose many of the species they have used for medicine, rituals and more, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Flávia Milhorance
item.media:credit: Victor Moriyama for The New York Times
item.media:description: The study warned that major losses of plant species could occur in the coming decades even if nations limit global warming to moderate levels.

item.title: At End of NATO Summit, Leaders Find Much to Agree on Despite Trump’s Insults
item.description: The alliance restated its commitment to collective defense and reiterated its support for Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.media:description: A declaration signed by the leaders of all NATO members on Wednesday affirmed a commitment to collective defense and reiterated the alliance’s support for Ukraine.

item.title: Recall Issued for Pedigree Dog Food Sold Despite Failing Quality Control
item.description: Mars Petcare recalled cans of dog food that might contain plastic or metal, which it said were supposed to be destroyed but ended up in stores.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: A file photo of Pedigree dog food. It does not depict the type that was recalled.

item.title: Trump Lashes Out at Spain: ‘Cut Off All Trade.’ Here’s Why That’s Unlikely.
item.description: A move against Spain would face political, legal and practical issues and probably provoke a wider trade war with the European Union. It could also roil markets.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump makes remarks as he meets with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the NATO meetings in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Iran targets sites in Bahrain and Kuwait.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Sanam Mahoozi
item.media:description: The outskirts of Kuwait City on Wednesday. Iran said it had targeted U.S. military sites in Kuwait.

item.title: Zelensky is to meet with Trump after a brutal week of Russian attacks on Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Cleaning up at the remains of a residential building in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Tuesday after a Russian missile attack.

item.title: Tensions Escalate Between Russia and NATO’s European Members
item.description: Moscow has directed extra hostility toward countries that have strongly supported Ukraine and praise at President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.media:description: President Vladimir Putin of Russia in St. Petersburg in June.

item.title: Iran’s holy city of Mashhad prepares for Khamenei’s burial.
item.description: As t
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inside the arrivals terminal at Mashhad airport in Iran on Wednesday. Dozens of mostly pink and blue backpacks have been arranged as a memorial to the children killed when a strike hit an elementary school in Minab.

item.title: Denmark Says It Will Defend ‘Every Inch’ of NATO as Trump Eyes Greenland Again
item.description: After President Trump once more said that the island should be under U.S. control, the Danish prime minister, Mette Frederiksen, told reporters, “We will defend the kingdom of Denmark.”
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen of Denmark at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday. “We are ready to defend every inch of NATO, including our own territory,” she said.

item.title: Pakistan Finds Wreckage of Cargo Plane That Vanished at Sea Overnight
item.description: Pakistan’s Navy is still searching for missing crew members after the aircraft plunged vertically and went missing over the Arabian Sea, aviation authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.media:description: At Jinnah International Airport, in Karachi, Pakistan.

item.title: Trump Says He Will Grant Ukraine the License to Make Patriot Missiles, a Key Demand
item.description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine said before a NATO summit this week that he wanted Patriot missiles and the related defense systems to counter Russian ballistic missiles. But questions remained about President Trump’s pledge.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall, Stanislav Kozliuk and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Volodymyr Zelensky attending a meeting with the German Chancellor in Ankara, Turkey on Tuesday.

item.title: For Rebels in Congo, Ebola Could Help Strengthen Their Rule
item.description: A violent militia that controls large swaths of Congolese territory in the east may use the virus to try to bolster its authority.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean and Justin Makangara
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A church member distributes food to displaced people at the Kigonze camp. The camp is home to more than 25,000 people in Bunia, Ituri province, the epicenter of the Ebola outbreak in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, in June.

item.title: A Look Back at Trump’s Broadsides Against NATO Allies
item.description: President Trump has rarely missed an opportunity to castigate the Western military alliance, whose leaders he’s meeting this week, as weak and ineffective.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has consistently questioned one of NATO’s core provisions: the commitment laid out in Article 5 of its founding charter to treat an attack on one member as an attack on all.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard, Steven Erlanger, Tyler Pager, Shawn McCreesh and Lara Jakes

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After Trump Says Deal With Iran Is ‘Over’
item.description: Attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz sparked a new cycle of retaliation that could throttle the flow of energy from the region.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Tyler Pager and Shirin Hakim

item.title: Germans Don’t Love Budweiser. It Won’t Take No for an Answer.
item.description: Not for the first time, Budweiser is trying to make it in Germany. But this is a tough time to sell German drinkers on the image and taste of American beer.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: A box of Anheuser-Busch Bud beer at a supermarket in Berlin.

item.title: What to Know About Australia’s Contest With China in the Pacific After Beijing Tested a Missile
item.description: With a series of recent treaties and alliances, Canberra is seeking to prevent Beijing from establishing a military foothold in the Pacific Ocean.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese of Australia, left, and his counterpart from Fiji, Sitiveni Rabuka, signed two security agreements, in Suva, Fiji, on Monday.

item.title: India Calls Out Meta Over Reported Child Sexual Abuse Imagery in Ads
item.description: The tech giant that owns Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp says it is in touch with the Indian authorities about reports that exploitative content is being hawked on one of its platforms.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: A Meta logo at a conference in Mumbai, India.

item.title: In Israel, Rahm Emanuel Calls for End of Unconditional U.S. Support
item.description: The former Chicago mayor, a Democrat exploring a 2028 presidential run, delivered a speech in Tel Aviv that was sharply critical of Benjamin Netanyahu.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:description: Rahm Emanuel, the former Chicago mayor and White House chief of staff, is trying to chart a course between the anti-Israel left and the pro-Israel right.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran and Reimposes Sanctions After Tanker Attacks
item.description: The military operation came hours after the U.S. Treasury revoked a waiver allowing global sales of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Negotiations between Iran and the United States have been paused until after multiday funeral ceremonies in Tehran for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader who was killed on the first day of the war.

item.title: Zimbabwe’s President Signs Law Extending His Term
item.description: The measure will allow the president, Emmerson Mnangagwa, to stay in office until 2030. Critics say the move tightens his autocratic grip on power and erodes the nation’s democracy.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Emmerson Mnangagwa of Zimbabwe at the United Nations headquarters in New York City in 2022.

item.title: A Plan to Stop the Far Right
item.description: The German chancellor and his center-left coalition partners are trying to hold off populist challengers. And they have a road map.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:description: Germany’s chancellor, Friedrich Merz, and his coalition partners are trying to hold off far-right challengers.

item.title: Marine Le Pen’s Court Battle Is Latest Clash Between Populists and Watchdogs
item.description: The far-right leader’s battle with the French judiciary evokes standoffs across the populist world, including ones with President Trump and Nigel Farage.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen leaving court in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: The Symbols We Saw at Khamenei’s Funeral
item.description: Abdi Latif Dahir, a Middle East correspondent for The Times, explains the messages behind recurring symbols at the dayslong funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Michael Anthony Adams, Mikhail Galustov, Rebecca Suner, Jon Hazell, Joey Sendaydiego and Rafaela Balster
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: I.O.C. Lifts Russia’s Olympic Suspension, Clearing the Way for 2028
item.description: The International Olympic Committee “provisionally” removed the ban imposed in relation to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. A previous ban for systematic doping had already ended.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: The Russian Olympic Committee building in Moscow on Tuesday.

item.title: Venezuelans Criticize Own Government Response After Twin Earthquakes
item.description: Venezuelans are publicly expressing their anger over their government’s disaster response, despite the risk of imprisonment.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People look for their relatives in the rubble of a public housing complex in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday, after twin earthquakes destroyed it.

item.title: Man Arrested for Painting ‘Pam the Bird’ Graffiti on Bridge Tower in Melbourne
item.description: An hourslong standoff with the police ended after the man descended the 140-foot tower and surrendered. The graffiti resembled the “Pam the Bird” symbol which began appearing across the city in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:credit: Joel Carrett/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: A man scaled a 140-foot concrete Melbourne bridge tower, abseiling down its side to paint a massive cartoon bird.

item.title: A Supreme Leader’s Final Crowds
item.description: What you could see when Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin was being driven to a public space in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Farage Says He Will Resign From UK’s Parliament, Setting Off a By-Election
item.description: Nigel Farage, leader of the populist right-wing party Reform U.K., has come under increasing pressure after a series of revelations about undisclosed gifts and donations.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

item.title: Prince Harry Loses Privacy Lawsuit Against Daily Mail Publisher
item.description: The case brought by Harry and other celebrities against Associated Newspapers was one of several legal battles that the prince has fought against British tabloids in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Prince Harry arriving at a court in London, in January. He and other celebrities had accused Associated Newspapers, the publisher of the Daily Mail, of intruding into their private lives.

item.title: Suspect in Monaco Bombing Attack Is Found Dead in Ukraine
item.description: The Ukrainian authorities said that Anastasiia Berezovska, who officials said was behind the explosion last week, had been shot.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Stanislav Kozliuk

item.title: Belgium’s World Cup Win Over U.S. Fuels Triumph and Snark in Europe
item.description: President Trump had pushed to allow an American player to take the field despite a red card suspension, and the trouncing of the U.S. team was painted as a sign of justice.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Koba Ryckewaert
item.media:description: Belgium fans celebrating their team’s 4-1 World Cup win against the United States in Seattle on Monday.
